impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that those products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act by conditioning licenses to its '844 patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the district court's determination that whether the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question, and affirmed the damages award. This case clarifies that patent holders cannot leverage invalid patents to impose tying arrangements on downstream products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses accused of patent infringement through tying arrangements can challenge the arrangement as anticompetitive, especially where the tying patent is invalid or the tied product has substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that condition patent licenses on exclusive purchase of related but unpatented products face significant antitrust liability risk, including damages, even where they believe the tied products lack substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the claims were obvious over prior art references.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's patent on streaming partially encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision directly impacts DRM and adaptive bitrate streaming technologies used widely in the entertainment and technology industries.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against patent assertions in the streaming and DRM space benefit from the Federal Circuit's willingness to correct Board claim constructions that improperly narrow prior art applicability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in digital media streaming technologies face increased vulnerability to IPR challenges where claim terms are broadly construed to encompass more prior art.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-based methods for tailoring content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods of tailoring streamed content to device capabilities are ineligible for patenting under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement verdict and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to abstract ideas despite the district court's initial determination that they survived a § 101 challenge. This decision narrows patent protection for adaptive content delivery technologies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Major streaming and technology companies gain protection from patent assertions based on abstract content-delivery tailoring methods, reducing litigation exposure in the adaptive streaming space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in content delivery and adaptive streaming technologies face heightened § 101 eligibility challenges, potentially invalidating portfolios directed to device-specific content tailoring.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities state a plausible claim of patent infringement sufficient to survive a Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of a patent infringement complaint against Google for failure to state a claim, finding the plaintiff's allegations insufficient to plausibly map the patent's web conferencing claims onto Google's products. This decision reinforces the pleading standards for patent infringement complaints under Twombly/Iqbal.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from robust pleading requirements that can dispose of weak patent infringement claims at the motion to dismiss stage, reducing litigation costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face a higher bar in drafting infringement complaints, as courts may dismiss claims that fail to plausibly map patent limitations to accused products.

impacts.caseName: Seagen Inc. v. Daiichi Sankyo Company, Ltd.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) with tetrapeptide linkers satisfied the written description and enablement requirements under 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's denial of JMOL, finding Seagen's ADC patent invalid for lack of written description and enablement despite a jury verdict of willful infringement and over $41 million in damages. The decision has major implications for the pharmaceutical and biotech industries, particularly for the scope of patent claims covering broad classes of therapeutic compounds.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech companies defending against broad genus patent claims benefit from heightened written description and enablement requirements, potentially freeing competitive drug development pathways.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the life sciences face significant risk that genus claims covering broad classes of compounds will be invalidated for insufficient written description or enablement, undermining patent portfolio value.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding discretionary IPR non-institution decisions qualify as general policy statements exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions. This preserves the PTO's flexibility in managing its IPR docket.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained discretion to deny IPR institution, as challengers cannot force procedural constraints through APA rulemaking requirements.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners face continued uncertainty about discretionary denials, as the PTO's non-institution guidance need not go through formal rulemaking and remains largely unreviewable.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an assignee of common law trademark rights can establish priority over a federal registrant's mark through a valid assignment of goodwill, and whether failure to submit evidence at trial warrants dismissal of a trademark opposition.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's registration for 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' and dismissal of its opposition to UNIP's marks, finding UNIP established priority through a valid asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and related goodwill. Game Plan's failure to submit any evidence at trial was fatal to its claims. This case underscores the importance of common law trademark rights acquired through valid assignments.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies acquiring trademark rights through asset purchases benefit from clarity that a valid assignment of marks with associated goodwill can establish priority over existing federal registrations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants who fail to actively prosecute opposition proceedings or submit evidence risk cancellation of their registrations, even against later-acquired common law rights.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the doctrine of foreign equivalents renders the mark KAHWA generic or merely descriptive for cafés and coffee shops based on its meaning in Arabic or as a Kashmiri green tea.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops, finding insufficient evidence that the foreign-language meaning of the term rendered it generic or merely descriptive of the applicant's services. The decision limits the PTO's ability to apply the doctrine of foreign equivalents where record evidence does not support consumer understanding of the foreign term's meaning in the relevant market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses seeking to register foreign-language marks benefit from a higher evidentiary bar the PTO must meet to refuse registration under the doctrine of foreign equivalents, particularly for terms with multiple foreign meanings.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an agreement between a defense contractor and the government regarding inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool for cost-plus government contracts is valid under the Cost Accounting Standards.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an agreement between Pratt & Whitney and the government regarding inclusion of Drag costs in the overhead pool was invalid, reversed the Board's decision on that issue, and dismissed the government's appeal on the overhead allocation base as non-final. The case addresses how defense contractors allocate indirect costs between government and commercial contracts under the Cost Accounting Standards, with significant implications for government contracting practices.
impacts.positiveImplications: The government benefits from invalidation of cost agreements that may have inflated overhead charges, potentially reducing costs on cost-plus defense contracts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors relying on negotiated cost agreements face risk that such agreements may be invalidated, potentially requiring retroactive cost adjustments and impacting profitability of government contracts.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with 19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a and 1677f, and whether such practice abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing statute and does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements rather than blanket designations. This decision increases transparency in trade remedy proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries and the public benefit from increased transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, as companies can no longer expect blanket confidential treatment of questionnaire responses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign manufacturers and importers participating in trade investigations face increased risk that business information submitted in questionnaire responses may become public, potentially exposing competitively sensitive data.

impacts.caseName: Wonderland Switzerland AG v. Evenflo Company, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a jury's finding of infringement under the doctrine of equivalents was supported by substantial evidence for patents directed to child car seat designs, and whether the district court properly entered a permanent injunction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed in part and reversed in part a judgment finding Evenflo's car seats infringed Wonderland's patents on child car seat attachment mechanisms, addressing both literal infringement and doctrine of equivalents. The court also reviewed the scope of a permanent injunction covering both asserted patents. The decision impacts product design freedom in the child safety products market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer product design benefit from the court's willingness to uphold infringement findings and permanent injunctions, providing strong enforcement tools.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer product manufacturers face ongoing risk of broad permanent injunctions that may cover product lines beyond those specifically found to infringe, requiring careful design-around strategies.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Circuit has appellate jurisdiction over a district court order imposing a bond under the Idaho Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act against patent assertion entities.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed for lack of jurisdiction an appeal from an $8 million bond imposed under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act against patent assertion entities Longhorn IP and Katana Silicon Technologies that had sued Micron for patent infringement. While the jurisdictional dismissal limits the precedential impact, the underlying case highlights the growing use of state anti-patent-troll statutes to deter bad faith patent assertions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses targeted by patent assertion entities can leverage state bad faith patent assertion statutes to impose significant bond requirements, deterring frivolous patent claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities and patent holders more broadly face financial barriers from state anti-troll statutes that may chill legitimate patent enforcement through bond requirements.

impacts.caseName: Adnexus Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent infringement complaint alleging infringement of an online advertising method patent was properly dismissed under Rule 12(b)(6) for failure to state a claim.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the dismissal of Adnexus's patent infringement complaint against Meta's Lead Ads product and remanded for further proceedings, finding that the district court improperly applied the pleading standards for patent infringement. This decision provides guidance on the sufficiency of patent infringement complaints, particularly regarding claim charts and infringement contentions appended to complaints.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from clarification that infringement complaints accompanied by claim charts and infringement contentions may survive Rule 12(b)(6) dismissal, preserving access to discovery.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies may face increased difficulty in obtaining early dismissal of patent infringement suits where plaintiffs attach detailed claim charts to their complaints.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether substantial evidence supported the jury's finding that claims directed to pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical connections were not obvious, and whether the damages award was proper.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL motion on obviousness and upheld the damages award for Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with simultaneous mechanical-electrical connections. The decision reinforces deference to jury findings on obviousness where substantial evidence supports the verdict, and validates the damages methodology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer product industries benefit from the court's strong deference to jury determinations on non-obviousness and damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors face difficulty overturning jury verdicts on obviousness at the JMOL stage, even where prior art references appear closely related to the patented invention.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development S.R.O. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly granted judgment as a matter of law overturning a $64 million jury verdict for trade secret misappropriation related to self-inflating tire technology, and whether correction of inventorship was properly denied.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed JMOL overturning a $64 million jury verdict ($2.8M compensatory, $61.2M punitive) for trade secret misappropriation of self-inflating tire technology, finding Goodyear was entitled to judgment as a matter of law. The court also upheld denial of correction of inventorship for a related patent. This decision demonstrates the high evidentiary bar for maintaining trade secret misappropriation verdicts on appeal.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of trade secret misappropriation benefit from appellate courts' willingness to overturn large jury verdicts where evidence of misappropriation is insufficient as a matter of law.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret owners face significant risk that even substantial jury verdicts may be overturned on JMOL, underscoring the need for precise identification and proof of trade secret misappropriation.
